narration.default: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.

Every C-shape block has a SLOT in the middle — that is the content slot. Whatever you put in the slot is the STUFF between the open tag and the close tag. Today we will see what happens when blocks go INSIDE other blocks slots. That is called nesting.
Drag a heading inside the body. Keep level 1 and type Bold, Italic, Underline.
Drag the b block below the heading inside the body. Drag a text block inside the b and type This is bold text. Whatever sits inside b becomes thicker on the page.
Drag an i block below the b block, still inside the body. Drag a text block inside the i and type This is italic text. Italic makes the letters slant.
Drag a u block below the i block, still inside the body. Drag a text block inside the u and type This is underlined text. The u block draws a line under whatever is inside.

Drag a paragraph below the underlined text. Inside the paragraph, drag a b block. Inside the b, drag an i block. Inside the i, drag a text block and type "This is bold AND italic." Tags inside tags COMBINE — the page shows both effects on the same words.
Now nest a u inside the i. Same words inside become bold, italic, AND underlined. The deeper you nest, the more effects pile on.
Click the View tab. Do you see one bold line, one italic line, and one underlined line?

Here's something you might have noticed. Your bold text and italic text sat on the SAME LINE as the other words. But your paragraphs and headings each started on a NEW line. Why the difference? HTML elements come in two flavours. INLINE elements (like b, i, u, a) sit in the flow of words like extra colour or emphasis — they don't break the line. BLOCK elements (like p, h1, div) each take a whole line by themselves — the next thing has to start below them. Most of the time you don't have to think about it; the browser does the right thing automatically. Just know the difference exists — it explains why some things wrap into the line and others jump down.
Use b for bold, i for italic, and u for underlined. There are also strong and em tags that look the same as b and i but tell screen readers the words are important.
